<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="edit_file_v2">

<details><summary>Tool use: **edit_file_v2** • Edit file: /Users/devendervutukuru/Desktop/My Projects/Visioryx/backend/app/api/users.py</summary>

</details>

</tool-use>

---



---


Summary of changes:

## 1. Syntax error in `api.ts`

The JSDoc comment contained `*/` inside `stream/*/mjpeg`, which closed the block comment early and caused a parse error.

**Change:** Updated the comment to avoid `*/` (e.g. "Use for stream mjpeg endpoints only").

## 2. Database storage verification

Data flow is wired correctly:

| Data | Storage | Flow |
|------|---------|------|
| **Face embeddings (registered users)** | `users.face_embedding` (ARRAY) | Upload → `extract_embeddings_from_image` → `user.face_embedding` → `db.flush()` → `get_db` commits |
| **Face detections** | `detections` table | `annotate_frame` → `enqueue_detection` → `log_detection` → `db.add(Detection)` + `db.commit()` |
| **Unknown faces** | `unknown_faces` table | Unknown face → `_save_unknown_face_crop` → `enqueue_detection` with embedding → `_log_unknown_face` → `db.add(UnknownFace)` + `db.commit()` |
| **Object detections** | `objects` table | `detect_objects` → `enqueue_object_detection` → `log_object_detection` → `db.add(ObjectDetection)` + `db.commit()` |
| **Alerts** | `alerts` table | `create_alert` → `db.add(Alert)` + `db.commit()` |

## 3. Improvements made

- **Detection bbox:** Face bounding boxes are now passed through and stored in `Detection.bbox` (JSONB).
- **Queue handling:** Queue processing updated to support the new bbox field and remain compatible with older queue items.

Restart the dev server; the syntax error should be gone and the app should compile.

| Web search results for query: Next.js 15 migration from 14 breaking changes |  | Links:<br/>1. [Upgrading: Version 15 \| Next.js](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/upgrading/version-15)<br/>2. [Breaking Changes in Next.js 15: The Ultimate Migration Guide \| by Amaresh Adak \| JavaScript in Plain English](https://javascript.plainenglish.io/breaking-changes-in-next-js-15-the-ultimate-migration-guide-18f183dad64b)<br/>3. [Next.js 15 \| Next.js](https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15)<br/>4. [Next.js 15 RC \| Next.js](https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-rc)<br/>5. [Next.js 15 RC 2 \| Next.js](https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-rc2)<br/><br/># Next.js 15 Migration from 14: Key Breaking Changes<br/><br/>## Main Breaking Changes<br/><br/>**Async Request APIs** [1][3]<br/>The most significant change is that previously synchronous APIs are now asynchronous:<br/>- `searchParams` in pages<br/>- `params` in layouts, pages, routes, and metadata files<br/>- `headers()`<br/>- `cookies()`<br/>- `draftMode()`<br/><br/>Example: `const cookieStore = await cookies()` instead of `const cookieStore = cookies()` [1]<br/><br/>A codemod is available to automate this migration, or you can temporarily use synchronous access with `UnsafeUnwrappedCookies`. [1]<br/><br/>**Caching Semantics** [3]<br/>- `fetch` requests, `GET` Route Handlers, and client navigations are no longer cached by default<br/><br/>## React 19 Requirement [1]<br/>- React 19 is now the default and minimum version for App Router<br/>- `useFormState` is deprecated and replaced by `useActionState`<br/>- `useFormStatus` now includes additional keys like `data`, `method`, and `action`<br/><br/>## Upgrade Path [1][3]<br/><br/>Use the automated codemod CLI:<br/>```bash<br/>npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest<br/>```<br/><br/>Or manually update:<br/>```bash<br/>npm install next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest<br/>```<br/><br/>The codemod CLI handles dependency updates and guides you through applying transformations automatically. [1][3] |

</details>
</tool-use>

Result:

```json
{
  "url": "https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/upgrading/version-15",
  "markdown": "## Upgrading from 14 to 15\n\nTo update to Next.js version 15, you can use the &#96;upgrade&#96; codemod:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\npnpm dlx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nnpx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nyarn dlx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nbunx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nIf you prefer to do it manually, ensure that you're installing the latest Next &amp; React versions:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\npnpm add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nnpm install next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nyarn add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nbun add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&gt; - If you see a peer dependencies warning, you may need to update &#96;react&#96; and &#96;react-dom&#96; to the suggested versions, or use the &#96;--force&#96; or &#96;--legacy-peer-deps&#96; flag to ignore the warning. This won't be necessary once both Next.js 15 and React 19 are stable.\n&gt; \n&gt; Good to know:\n\n## React 19\n\n- Read more in the [React 19 upgrade guide](https://react.dev/blog/2024/04/25/react-19-upgrade-guide).\n- &#96;useFormStatus&#96; now includes additional keys like &#96;data&#96;, &#96;method&#96;, and &#96;action&#96;. If you are not using React 19, only the &#96;pending&#96; key is available. [Learn more](https://react.dev/reference/react-dom/hooks/useFormStatus).\n- &#96;useFormState&#96; has been replaced by &#96;useActionState&#96;. The &#96;useFormState&#96; hook is still available in React 19, but it is deprecated and will be removed in a future release. &#96;useActionState&#96; is recommended and includes additional properties like reading the &#96;pending&#96; state directly. [Learn more](https://react.dev/reference/react/useActionState).\n- The minimum versions of &#96;react&#96; and &#96;react-dom&#96; is now 19.\n\n&gt; Good to know: If you are using TypeScript, ensure you also upgrade &#96;@types/react&#96; and &#96;@types/react-dom&#96; to their latest versions.\n\n## Async Request APIs (Breaking change)\n\nPreviously synchronous Dynamic APIs that rely on runtime information are now asynchronous:\n\n- &#96;searchParams&#96; in [&#96;page.js&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page)\n- &#96;params&#96; in [&#96;layout.js&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout), [&#96;page.js&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page), [&#96;route.js&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route), [&#96;default.js&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/default), [&#96;opengraph-image&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image), [&#96;twitter-image&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image), [&#96;icon&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons), and [&#96;apple-icon&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons).\n- [&#96;draftMode&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/draft-mode)\n- [&#96;headers&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers)\n- [&#96;cookies&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies)\n\nTo ease the burden of migration, a [codemod is available](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#150) to automate the process and the APIs can temporarily be accessed synchronously.\n\n### &#96;cookies&#96;\n\n#### Recommended Async Usage\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;tsx\nimport { cookies } from 'next/headers'\n\n// Before\nconst cookieStore = cookies()\nconst token = cookieStore.get('token')\n\n// After\nconst cookieStore = await cookies()\nconst token = cookieStore.get('token')\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Temporary Synchronous Usage\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;tsx\nimport { cookies, type UnsafeUnwrappedCookies } from 'next/headers'\n\n// Before\nconst cookieStore = cookies()\nconst token = cookieStore.get('token')\n\n// After\nconst cookieStore = cookies() as unknown as UnsafeUnwrappedCookies\n// will log a warning in dev\nconst token = cookieStore.get('token')\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;jsx\nimport { cookies } from 'next/headers'\n\n// Before\nconst cookieStore = cookies()\nconst token = cookieStore.get('token')\n\n// After\nconst cookieStore = cookies()\n// will log a warning in dev\nconst token = cookieStore.get('token')\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### &#96;headers&#96;\n\n#### Recommended Async Usage\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;tsx\nimport { headers } from 'next/headers'\n\n// Before\nconst headersList = headers()\nconst userAgent = headersList.get('user-agent')\n\n// After\nconst headersList = await headers()\nconst userAgent = headersList.get('user-agent')\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Temporary Synchronous Usage\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;tsx\nimport { headers, type UnsafeUnwrappedHeaders } from 'next/headers'\n\n// Before\nconst headersList = headers()\nconst userAgent = headersList.get('user-agent')\n\n// After\nconst headersList = headers() as unknown as UnsafeUnwrappedHeaders\n// will log a warning in dev\nconst userAgent = headersList.get('user-agent')\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;jsx\nimport { headers } from 'next/headers'\n\n// Before\nconst headersList = headers()\nconst userAgent = headersList.get('user-agent')\n\n// After\nconst headersList = headers()\n// will log a warning in dev\nconst userAgent = headersList.get('user-agent')\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### &#96;draftMode&#96;\n\n#### Recommended Async Usage\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;tsx\nimport { draftMode } from 'next/headers'\n\n// Before\nconst { isEnabled } = draftMode()\n\n// After\nconst { isEnabled } = await draftMode()\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Temporary Synchronous Usage\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;tsx\nimport { draftMode, type UnsafeUnwrappedDraftMode } from 'next/headers'\n\n// Before\nconst { isEnabled } = draftMode()\n\n// After\n// will log a warning in dev\nconst { isEnabled } = draftMode() as unknown as UnsafeUnwrappedDraftMode\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;jsx\nimport { draftMode } from 'next/headers'\n\n// Before\nconst { isEnabled } = draftMode()\n\n// After\n// will log a warning in dev\nconst { isEnabled } = draftMode()\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### &#96;params&#96; &amp; &#96;searchParams&#96;\n\n#### Asynchronous Layout\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;tsx\n// Before\ntype Params = { slug: string }\n\nexport function generateMetadata({ params }: { params: Params }) {\n  const { slug } = params\n}\n\nexport default async function Layout({\n  children,\n  params,\n}: {\n  children: React.ReactNode\n  params: Params\n}) {\n  const { slug } = params\n}\n\n// After\ntype Params = Promise&lt;{ slug: string }&gt;\n\nexport async function generateMetadata({ params }: { params: Params }) {\n  const { slug } = await params\n}\n\nexport default async function Layout({\n  children,\n  params,\n}: {\n  children: React.ReactNode\n  params: Params\n}) {\n  const { slug } = await params\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;jsx\n// Before\nexport function generateMetadata({ params }) {\n  const { slug } = params\n}\n\nexport default async function Layout({ children, params }) {\n  const { slug } = params\n}\n\n// After\nexport async function generateMetadata({ params }) {\n  const { slug } = await params\n}\n\nexport default async function Layout({ children, params }) {\n  const { slug } = await params\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Synchronous Layout\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;tsx\n// Before\ntype Params = { slug: string }\n\nexport default function Layout({\n  children,\n  params,\n}: {\n  children: React.ReactNode\n  params: Params\n}) {\n  const { slug } = params\n}\n\n// After\nimport { use } from 'react'\n\ntype Params = Promise&lt;{ slug: string }&gt;\n\nexport default function Layout(props: {\n  children: React.ReactNode\n  params: Params\n}) {\n  const params = use(props.params)\n  const slug = params.slug\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;jsx\n// Before\nexport default function Layout({ children, params }) {\n  const { slug } = params\n}\n\n// After\nimport { use } from 'react'\nexport default async function Layout(props) {\n  const params = use(props.params)\n  const slug = params.slug\n}\n\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Asynchronous Page\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;tsx\n// Before\ntype Params = { slug: string }\ntype SearchParams = { [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }\n\nexport function generateMetadata({\n  params,\n  searchParams,\n}: {\n  params: Params\n  searchParams: SearchParams\n}) {\n  const { slug } = params\n  const { query } = searchParams\n}\n\nexport default async function Page({\n  params,\n  searchParams,\n}: {\n  params: Params\n  searchParams: SearchParams\n}) {\n  const { slug } = params\n  const { query } = searchParams\n}\n\n// After\ntype Params = Promise&lt;{ slug: string }&gt;\ntype SearchParams = Promise&lt;{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }&gt;\n\nexport async function generateMetadata(props: {\n  params: Params\n  searchParams: SearchParams\n}) {\n  const params = await props.params\n  const searchParams = await props.searchParams\n  const slug = params.slug\n  const query = searchParams.query\n}\n\nexport default async function Page(props: {\n  params: Params\n  searchParams: SearchParams\n}) {\n  const params = await props.params\n  const searchParams = await props.searchParams\n  const slug = params.slug\n  const query = searchParams.query\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;jsx\n// Before\nexport function generateMetadata({ params, searchParams }) {\n  const { slug } = params\n  const { query } = searchParams\n}\n\nexport default function Page({ params, searchParams }) {\n  const { slug } = params\n  const { query } = searchParams\n}\n\n// After\nexport async function generateMetadata(props) {\n  const params = await props.params\n  const searchParams = await props.searchParams\n  const slug = params.slug\n  const query = searchParams.query\n}\n\nexport async function Page(props) {\n  const params = await props.params\n  const searchParams = await props.searchParams\n  const slug = params.slug\n  const query = searchParams.query\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Synchronous Page\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;tsx\n'use client'\n\n// Before\ntype Params = { slug: string }\ntype SearchParams = { [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }\n\nexport default function Page({\n  params,\n  searchParams,\n}: {\n  params: Params\n  searchParams: SearchParams\n}) {\n  const { slug } = params\n  const { query } = searchParams\n}\n\n// After\nimport { use } from 'react'\n\ntype Params = Promise&lt;{ slug: string }&gt;\ntype SearchParams = Promise&lt;{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }&gt;\n\nexport default function Page(props: {\n  params: Params\n  searchParams: SearchParams\n}) {\n  const params = use(props.params)\n  const searchParams = use(props.searchParams)\n  const slug = params.slug\n  const query = searchParams.query\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;jsx\n// Before\nexport default function Page({ params, searchParams }) {\n  const { slug } = params\n  const { query } = searchParams\n}\n\n// After\nimport { use } from \"react\"\n\nexport default function Page(props) {\n  const params = use(props.params)\n  const searchParams = use(props.searchParams)\n  const slug = params.slug\n  const query = searchParams.query\n}\n\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Route Handlers\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;tsx\n// Before\ntype Params = { slug: string }\n\nexport async function GET(request: Request, segmentData: { params: Params }) {\n  const params = segmentData.params\n  const slug = params.slug\n}\n\n// After\ntype Params = Promise&lt;{ slug: string }&gt;\n\nexport async function GET(request: Request, segmentData: { params: Params }) {\n  const params = await segmentData.params\n  const slug = params.slug\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\n// Before\nexport async function GET(request, segmentData) {\n  const params = segmentData.params\n  const slug = params.slug\n}\n\n// After\nexport async function GET(request, segmentData) {\n  const params = await segmentData.params\n  const slug = params.slug\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## &#96;runtime&#96; configuration (Breaking change)\n\nThe &#96;runtime&#96; [segment configuration](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config#runtime) previously supported a value of &#96;experimental-edge&#96; in addition to &#96;edge&#96;. Both configurations refer to the same thing, and to simplify the options, we will now error if &#96;experimental-edge&#96; is used. To fix this, update your &#96;runtime&#96; configuration to &#96;edge&#96;. A [codemod](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#app-dir-runtime-config-experimental-edge) is available to automatically do this.\n\n## &#96;fetch&#96; requests\n\n[&#96;fetch&#96; requests](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch) are no longer cached by default.\n\nTo opt specific &#96;fetch&#96; requests into caching, you can pass the &#96;cache: 'force-cache'&#96; option.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nexport default async function RootLayout() {\n  const a = await fetch('https://...') // Not Cached\n  const b = await fetch('https://...', { cache: 'force-cache' }) // Cached\n\n  // ...\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nTo opt all &#96;fetch&#96; requests in a layout or page into caching, you can use the &#96;export const fetchCache = 'default-cache'&#96; [segment config option](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config). If individual &#96;fetch&#96; requests specify a &#96;cache&#96; option, that will be used instead.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\n// Since this is the root layout, all fetch requests in the app\n// that don't set their own cache option will be cached.\nexport const fetchCache = 'default-cache'\n\nexport default async function RootLayout() {\n  const a = await fetch('https://...') // Cached\n  const b = await fetch('https://...', { cache: 'no-store' }) // Not cached\n\n  // ...\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Route Handlers\n\n&#96;GET&#96; functions in [Route Handlers](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) are no longer cached by default. To opt &#96;GET&#96; methods into caching, you can use a [route config option](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config) such as &#96;export const dynamic = 'force-static'&#96; in your Route Handler file.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nexport const dynamic = 'force-static'\n\nexport async function GET() {}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Client-side Router Cache\n\nWhen navigating between pages via &#96;&#96; or &#96;useRouter&#96;, [page](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) segments are no longer reused from the client-side router cache. However, they are still reused during browser backward and forward navigation and for shared layouts.\n\nTo opt page segments into caching, you can use the [&#96;staleTimes&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/staleTimes) config option:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\n/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */\nconst nextConfig = {\n  experimental: {\n    staleTimes: {\n      dynamic: 30,\n      static: 180,\n    },\n  },\n}\n\nmodule.exports = nextConfig\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n[Layouts](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout) and [loading states](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading) are still cached and reused on navigation.\n\n## &#96;next/font&#96;\n\nThe &#96;@next/font&#96; package has been removed in favor of the built-in [&#96;next/font&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/components/font). A [codemod is available](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#built-in-next-font) to safely and automatically rename your imports.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\n// Before\nimport { Inter } from '@next/font/google'\n\n// After\nimport { Inter } from 'next/font/google'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## bundlePagesRouterDependencies\n\n&#96;experimental.bundlePagesExternals&#96; is now stable and renamed to &#96;bundlePagesRouterDependencies&#96;.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\n/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */\nconst nextConfig = {\n  // Before\n  experimental: {\n    bundlePagesExternals: true,\n  },\n\n  // After\n  bundlePagesRouterDependencies: true,\n}\n\nmodule.exports = nextConfig\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## serverExternalPackages\n\n&#96;experimental.serverComponentsExternalPackages&#96; is now stable and renamed to &#96;serverExternalPackages&#96;.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\n/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */\nconst nextConfig = {\n  // Before\n  experimental: {\n    serverComponentsExternalPackages: ['package-name'],\n  },\n\n  // After\n  serverExternalPackages: ['package-name'],\n}\n\nmodule.exports = nextConfig\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Speed Insights\n\nAuto instrumentation for Speed Insights was removed in Next.js 15.\n\nTo continue using Speed Insights, follow the [Vercel Speed Insights Quickstart](https://vercel.com/docs/speed-insights/quickstart) guide.\n\n## &#96;NextRequest&#96; Geolocation\n\nThe &#96;geo&#96; and &#96;ip&#96; properties on &#96;NextRequest&#96; have been removed as these values are provided by your hosting provider. A [codemod](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#150) is available to automate this migration.\n\nIf you are using Vercel, you can alternatively use the &#96;geolocation&#96; and &#96;ipAddress&#96; functions from [&#96;@vercel/functions&#96;](https://vercel.com/docs/functions/vercel-functions-package) instead:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;ts\nimport { geolocation } from '@vercel/functions'\nimport type { NextRequest } from 'next/server'\n\nexport function middleware(request: NextRequest) {\n  const { city } = geolocation(request)\n\n  // ...\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;ts\nimport { ipAddress } from '@vercel/functions'\nimport type { NextRequest } from 'next/server'\n\nexport function middleware(request: NextRequest) {\n  const ip = ipAddress(request)\n\n  // ...\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n---\n\nFor a semantic overview of all documentation, see [/docs/sitemap.md](https://nextjs.org/docs/sitemap.md)\n\nFor an index of all available documentation, see [/docs/llms.txt](https://nextjs.org/docs/llms.txt)"
}

[React 19 support · Pull Request #15342 · mui/mui-x](https://github.com/mui/mui-x/pull/15342)<br/>3. [[internal] Bump React to 19 (2.0) · Pull Request #44672 · mui/material-ui](https://github.com/mui/material-ui/pull/44672)<br/>4. [[internal] Add React 19 as peer dependency · Pull Request #43216 · mui/material-ui](https://github.com/mui/material-ui/pull/43216)<br/><br/># MUI Material UI React 19 Compatibility<br/><br/>Material UI has successfully migrated to React 19 compatibility. Here's the status:<br/><br/>## Migration Status<br/><br/>MUI completed its React 19 migration in late 2024. The team used a two-phase approach:[1]<br/>1. First, they added React 19 compatibility while keeping the codebase on React 18<br/>2. Then, they moved the entire codebase to React 19 while maintaining backward compatibility with React 18<br/><br/>Material UI (the core library) now supports React 19, with React 19 added as a peer dependency.[5] MUI X components (Data Grid, Pickers, Charts, Tree View) also received React 19 support.[2]<br/><br/>## Key Changes Required<br/><br/>The migration required relatively minimal source code changes, but significant test updates were needed due to:[1]<br/>- **Strict mode improvements** in React 19 that changed component call counts<br/>- **Error reporting changes** that modified console output<br/>- **Ref handling changes** - React 19 allows refs as props on function components, eliminating the need for `forwardRef` in some cases<br/><br/>## Backward Compatibility<br/><br/>MUI maintained support for React 18 applications, allowing users to upgrade on their own timeline without being forced to migrate immediately.[1] The library supports both React versions simultaneously.<br/><br/>## Bundle Size Improvements<br/><br/>The React 19 migration resulted in modest bundle size reductions across components, with some showing up to 1.34% improvements in parsed sizes.[3] |

[fix: expose HttpProxy options to next config · Pull Request #40277 · vercel/next.js](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/40277)<br/><br/># Next.js 15 proxyTimeout Experimental Config<br/><br/>Next.js provides an undocumented experimental `proxyTimeout` configuration option that allows you to customize the proxy timeout for rewrites.[1]<br/><br/>## Configuration<br/><br/>You can set the `proxyTimeout` in your `next.config.js` file under the `experimental` object:<br/><br/>```javascript<br/>experimental: \{<br/>  proxyTimeout: 1000 * 120, // 120 seconds, for example<br/>\}<br/>```<br/><br/>## Background<br/><br/>By default, Next.js sets a 30-second proxy timeout for rewrites, which can cause "socket hang up" errors when proxied servers take longer to respond.[3] This timeout was hardcoded, but the `experimental.proxyTimeout` option was added to allow users to override it.[1]<br/><br/>The feature was previously discussed in GitHub issues where users requested the ability to configure this value for use cases involving long-running API requests, such as file generation or database operations.[2][4]<br/><br/>**Note:** This feature remains undocumented in the official Next.js documentation, but it is actively used and functional.[1] |

Summary of changes:

## 1. WelcomeCard import fix

The dashboard was using `WelcomeCard` and `FeaturedCard` without importing them. Added:

```ts
import { WelcomeCard, FeaturedCard } from '@/components/dashboard';
```

## 2. Frontend dependency updates

| Package | Before | After |
|---------|--------|-------|
| **Next.js** | 14.2.35 | **15.1.6** |
| **React** | 18.3.1 | **19.0.0** |
| **React-DOM** | 18.3.1 | **19.0.0** |
| **eslint-config-next** | 14.2.35 | **15.1.6** |
| **@mui/material** | 5.16.7 | **5.18.0** |
| **@mui/icons-material** | 5.16.7 | **5.18.0** |
| **@emotion/react** | 11.13.3 | **11.14.0** |
| **@emotion/styled** | 11.13.0 | **11.14.0** |
| **recharts** | 2.13.3 | **2.15.0** |
| **zustand** | 5.0.1 | **5.0.2** |
| **postcss** | 8.4.47 | **8.4.49** |
| **tailwindcss** | 3.4.14 | **3.4.16** |
| **typescript** | 5.6.3 | **5.7.2** |
| **@types/node** | 22.9.0 | **22.10.0** |
| **@types/react** | 18.3.12 | **19.0.0** |
| **@types/react-dom** | 18.3.1 | **19.0.0** |

## 3. Backend dependencies

Backend packages were already up to date; `requirements.txt` is unchanged.

## 4. Next steps

Install the updated frontend dependencies:

```bash
cd frontend && npm install
```

Then restart the dev server:

```bash
./scripts/start-dev.sh
```

**Note:** Next.js 15 uses React 19. MUI 5.18 supports React 19. If you hit peer dependency warnings, try `npm install --legacy-peer-deps`.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Handling RTSP stream errors**

I'm fixing the RTSP stream error handling to prevent clearing the frame buffer when stream ends. The stuck frame with static timestamp is likely from the camera's timestamp overlay, not a streaming issue. The stream may be delivering old/cached frames rather than stopping, explaining the black areas and office content instead of 'No signal'. 

The black bars are likely due to objectFit: 'contain' creating letterboxing; I'll switch to 'cover' to eliminate them. </details></think>

**Handling retry loop**

I'm ensuring the retry loop respects stop_event to prevent infinite reconnection attempts. I'm ensuring _frame_buffer pop occurs correctly when stopping the thread via stop_event. I'm adjusting the retry loop to check stop_event more frequently by using wait(1) with periodic checks. </details></think>

Summary of changes:

## 1. Dashboard cards

- **Stat cards**
  - Decorative circles reduced from 120px to 80px
  - Opacity lowered (0.08 → 0.06)
  - Position adjusted so they stay inside the card
  - `isolation: 'isolate'` added to avoid overlap
  - Content given `zIndex: 1` so it stays above the circles

- **Featured card**
  - Overlay circles reduced (180px → 120px, 120px → 80px)
  - `isolation: 'isolate'` added
  - Content given `zIndex: 1`

## 2. Live feed

- **Display**
  - `objectFit: 'cover'` so the video fills the container and removes black bars
  - Stream area height increased (200–280px → 220–320px)
  - Fullscreen dialog set to full viewport (100vw × 100vh)
  - Black background for the stream container

- **Reconnect**
  - "Reconnect" button added next to zoom controls to restart the stream when it freezes
  - Stream `key` updated to force a fresh load when reconnecting

- **Backend**
  - RTSP capture now auto-reconnects when the stream drops
  - Up to 5 retries on initial connect
  - When the stream ends, it waits 2 seconds and reopens the RTSP connection
  - "No signal" frame shown while reconnecting

## 3. Next steps

Restart the backend and frontend so the changes apply. If the feed still freezes:

1. Use the **Reconnect** button on the stream.
2. Check that the RTSP URL is correct and reachable from the backend.
3. Confirm the camera is on the same network as Visioryx (or use VPN for remote access).

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Analyzing hydration error**

The hydration error occurs because the drawer's collapsed state differs between server (false) and client (true from localStorage), causing a width mismatch during render. The sidebar renders differently between server and client because Next.js server-renders client components for initial HTML, causing hydration mismatch. The hydration mismatch likely stems from browser extension attributes like `cz-shortcut-listen` and `data-atm-ext-installed` injected into the DOM, which can cause mismatches between server and client-rendered HTML. 

**Fixing hydration error**

I'm adding suppressHydrationWarning to the root HTML element to fix the hydration mismatch caused by browser extension attributes. </details></think>

Summary of changes applied:

## Summary of Fixes

### 1. **Hydration error**
- Root layout already has `suppressHydrationWarning` on `<html>` and `<body>` to reduce hydration issues from browser extensions.
- Dashboard layout uses a `mounted` state so `localStorage` is only read after hydration.

### 2. **Date formatting (hydration)**
- Added `frontend/src/lib/formatDate.ts` using `date-fns` for consistent date formatting.
- Replaced `toLocaleString()` with `formatDateTime()` on:
  - Dashboard (Recent Detections, Recent Alerts)
  - Alerts page
  - Detections page  

This avoids hydration mismatches from different server/client locales.

### 3. **Dashboard cards**
- **Stat cards** (Total Users, Total Cameras, etc.): `bgcolor: 'background.paper'`, `minHeight: 160`, `isolation: 'isolate'`, smaller decorative circles.
- **Featured card** (Live Monitoring): `textShadow` for contrast, `color: 'rgba(255,255,255,0.95)'`, lower-opacity overlays.
- **Welcome card**: Dark gradient with clear text contrast.

### 4. **UI consistency**
- **Page headers**: All dashboard pages now use the same pattern:
  - `variant="h5" fontWeight={700}` for titles
  - `variant="body2" color="text.secondary"` for subtitles
- **Cards**: `bgcolor: 'background.paper'` and `CardContent sx={{ p: 3 }}` on:
  - Cameras, Users, Alerts, Detections, Analytics, Profile
- **Analytics**: Chart cards aligned with dashboard styling.

### 5. **Responsiveness**
- Grid breakpoints: `xs={12} sm={6} lg={3}` for stat cards; `xs={12} lg={8}` / `xs={12} lg={4}` for Welcome + Featured.
- Users page header uses `flexDirection: { xs: 'column', sm: 'row' }` for mobile.
- Layout uses `px: { xs: 1.5, sm: 2, md: 3 }` and safe-area insets for mobile.

### Files modified
- `frontend/src/lib/formatDate.ts` (new)
- `frontend/src/app/(dashboard)/dashboard/page.tsx`
- `frontend/src/app/(dashboard)/alerts/page.tsx`
- `frontend/src/app/(dashboard)/detections/page.tsx`
- `frontend/src/app/(dashboard)/analytics/page.tsx`
- `frontend/src/app/(dashboard)/cameras/page.tsx`
- `frontend/src/app/(dashboard)/users/page.tsx`
- `frontend/src/app/(dashboard)/profile/page.tsx`

Restart the dev server and refresh the dashboard. The hydration error should be reduced, and cards and text should render consistently across the app.

_**Agent (model default, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Patching the dashboard components**

I need to patch the StatCard component on the dashboard page. The FeaturedCard text appears too light, likely because of the gradient and circles. In the screenshot, the card background looks nearly white on a very light gradient. 

I’m checking whether the background property is not applying because it inherits from the default theme's Paper. It seems like the backgroundImage might be overriding it, so I need to investigate that further. It’s possible the quick access card is obscured because it’s behind the WelcomeCard. I’ll dig deeper!
**Investigating card component issues**

The issue could be that the Card component is using `component={Link}` from next/link. MUI requires the `component={Link}`, but I’m not sure if next/link is forwarding the ref correctly. 

It seems the styling might be applied to the nested `<a>` tag rather than the root. In Next 13+, Link renders an `<a>` inside. MUI expects `className`, but Next Link doesn’t accept `sx`. 

Maybe I should wrap `Link` as `component="a"` or use MUI's Link with `NextLinkBehavior`. Oddly, WelcomeCard works fine without it, while FeaturedCard and StatCard don’t. I'll need to check these!
**Analyzing link and card styles**

It appears that cards used as links lose their background and border because styles are applied to the Card, but Next Link doesn't render as a standard DOM element; it outputs an `<a>` child. When setting `component={Link}`, MUI treats Link as the root, effectively passing the `sx` prop to Next Link but not to the resulting `<a>`, which may cause styling issues.

A potential solution is to create a `NextLinkComposed` wrapper with `forwardRef` to pass props to the anchor. Alternatively, I could use the `legacyBehavior` option with Link. For a quick fix in StatCard and FeaturedCard, I might wrap with `<Link href=... passHref legacyBehavior>` and then use `<Card component="a">`. Let's implement this!
**Creating the LinkCard component**

I think it's better to create a `LinkCard` component that uses `next/link` and renders as `<Link href=... style={{textDecoration:'none'}}><Card ... component="div">...</Card></Link>`. However, I’m wondering if having the Card wrapped in the Link loses semantic meaning for being clickable, but it's okay, since I can set `component="a"`.

I realize that with `legacyBehavior` turned off, Link can wrap any child, and it will still render an `<a>`. Since the new Link expects a ReactNode and adds the `<a>` automatically, I can simply use `<Link href="/x" style={{textDecoration:'none'}}><Card ...>...</Card></Link>`, so the styles on the Card will indeed apply! 

I’ll modify the StatCard and FeaturedCard components to wrap their contents in a Link, remove `component` and `href` props, and also make sure that the Link's display style is set to block for clickable elements. I’ll check `dashboard/page.tsx` and `components/FeaturedCard.tsx` for any other patterns using `component={Link}`. Let's get to work on this!</details></think>

---

Got it — both the **Quick Access** card and the **stat cards** are rendering, but their **background/styles aren’t applying correctly** because they’re using `Card component={Link}`. I’m going to switch those to `Link` wrapping the `Card`, so MUI styling stays on the real DOM element, then tighten the card backgrounds/borders for clearer contrast.

### What I fixed for the screenshot

- **Stat cards (Total Users / Total Cameras / Today’s Detections / Unknown Today)**: changed from `Card component={Link}` to **`<Link><Card /></Link>`**, so the **Card background/border/shadow actually renders** (this was the main reason they looked “invisible”).
- **Quick Access (Live Monitoring) card**: same fix (**Link wraps Card**) plus a **subtle dark overlay** and stronger text contrast so the label isn’t washed out.

### Files updated
- `frontend/src/app/(dashboard)/dashboard/page.tsx` (StatCard)
- `frontend/src/components/dashboard/FeaturedCard.tsx` (Quick Access card)

Refresh `/dashboard` and you should see the cards with proper **visible backgrounds** and the Quick Access text readable.

_**User**_

python scripts were crashing is there a alternative way can we convert them to react only and fix it or is there any other best solution ?

---

_**Agent (model default, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Planning architecture options**

I’m thinking about how to address the user's request for alternatives in system architecture. It seems like we should inspect the Python scripts and propose options, like keeping the Python backend as a service and containerizing it. I notice monitoring probably requires Python OpenCV. React alone can’t handle RTSP easily, so I suggest keeping processing on the backend and running it as a separate service with checks. I wonder if we need crash details but won’t confirm assumptions, just ask for logs.</details></think>

---

React-only can’t replace those Python scripts if they’re doing **RTSP ingest / OpenCV / face recognition**. The browser (React) can *display* video, but it **cannot reliably pull RTSP** or run heavy CV pipelines with access to cameras the way a backend service can.

### Best practical solutions (ranked)

- **Best (recommended): split + harden Python as a service**
  - Run Python as a **separate long-running service** (Docker container / systemd / supervisord), not as a “script spawned by the web app”.
  - Add **auto-restart**, **health checks**, **structured logs**, and **crash dumps**.
  - Keep Next.js/React purely for UI + calling APIs + viewing streams.
  - This is the most stable + maintainable approach for production.

- **If your goal is only “view streams in React” (no AI in browser)**
  - Convert RTSP → **HLS** or **WebRTC** on the server (e.g., ffmpeg/GStreamer/MediaMTX), then React plays the stream via `<video>` (HLS) or WebRTC.
  - AI detection stays server-side (Python or another service).

- **Rewrite Python pipeline to Node/Go (possible, but usually not “better”)**
  - You’d still need a backend service for ingest + AI.
  - Rewriting is costly and often less mature than Python/OpenCV for CV.

### What I recommend we do next
1. Identify **which Python process** is crashing (stream worker, detector, ffmpeg wrapper, etc.).
2. Capture the crash output / logs and make it resilient (restart + isolate + monitor).
3. If you want, also implement **RTSP→HLS/WebRTC** so the React Live page is stable even if detection restarts.

If you paste the **terminal output / crash log** (or point me to the Python entry file), I’ll tell you exactly which option fits your current setup and implement the hardening path.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Evaluating backend strategies**

I discovered that the backend Python crashed, likely due to a segfault with macOS related to OpenCV or FFmpeg. To resolve this, I’m considering isolating the service using Docker or switching to an FFmpeg subprocess to eliminate the cv2 VideoCapture issue. Implementing an RTSP proxy with FFmpeg for HLS seems like a solid plan. The user prefers an alternative solution, so I'll focus on Dockerizing the service and using FFmpeg for streaming to reduce crash risks.</details></think>

---

I found the “streaming” is currently done inside the Python backend using `cv2.VideoCapture(..., cv2.CAP_FFMPEG)`, which is a common cause of **macOS Python hard crashes/segfaults** (OpenCV+FFmpeg bindings). Next I’ll refactor streaming to use **FFmpeg as an external process** (HLS) and run the backend as a **restartable service** so crashes don’t take down your UI.

---

### What I changed (to stop the Python stream crashes)

Your Live Monitoring was using **OpenCV `cv2.VideoCapture(...FFMPEG...)`** for RTSP → MJPEG. On macOS that often causes **hard Python crashes/segfaults** (exactly what your screenshot shows).

I implemented the recommended stable approach:

- **Backend streaming switched to HLS via FFmpeg subprocess (per camera)**  
  - New service: `backend/app/services/hls_manager.py`
  - New endpoints:
    - `GET /api/v1/stream/{camera_id}/hls/index.m3u8?token=...`
    - `GET /api/v1/stream/{camera_id}/hls/{segment}.ts?token=...`
  - `POST /api/v1/stream/{camera_id}/start|stop` now uses **HLS** when `STREAM_MODE=hls` (default)

- **Frontend Live page now plays HLS (with hls.js)**
  - Updated: `frontend/src/app/(dashboard)/live/page.tsx`
  - Added dependency: `hls.js` in `frontend/package.json`

### “Service mode” (auto-restart when Python dies)

Added a supervised runner that restarts the backend automatically if it crashes:

- New script: `scripts/start-backend-supervised.sh`  
- README updated to mention it.

### What you need to do now

1. **Install FFmpeg** (macOS):
   - `brew install ffmpeg`
2. Restart dev:
   - Backend (recommended): `./scripts/start-backend-supervised.sh`
   - Frontend: `./scripts/start-dev.sh frontend`
3. Open **Live Monitoring** again.

If a stream fails, check the per-camera log at:
- `backend/storage/hls/camera_<id>/ffmpeg.log`

**Addressing video issues**

I need to fix the "module not found" error by installing the hls.js dependency, but the earlier tool install was aborted. So, I'll run npm install in the frontend. There’s also a zoom issue: the zoom transform wrapper scales the video container, and it seems the fullscreen can't zoom out because the default zoom is set to 100. The user mentioned it’s defaulting zoomed in, possibly due to objectFit being "cover." Switching to "contain" in fullscreen could help. Additionally, I need to address the detection not showing up because the AI overlay may be disabled with the current HLS setup. Since our HLS pipeline just transcodes raw streams without detection overlay, I’ll seek alternatives to keep the detection separate. Using MJPEG for the overlay might be a good option, but the user expects person/object detection. There's a possibility to implement overlay in HLS by using the detection pipeline to generate overlayed frames into ffmpeg via a pipe, but that's quite heavy.
**Refining detection overlay**

I think I should keep the MJPEG overlay for detection to prevent crashes, but I'll avoid using cv2.VideoCapture. Instead, I can decode frames with ffmpeg and pipe them into numpy for object detection, which sounds complex. The user's request about not seeing detected persons or objects during live monitoring indicates that we need to address this issue. First, I'll fix the missing hls.js by running npm install and updating package.json. There’s also a webpack cache error due to a long path that I might ignore. I'll implement the MJPEG fallback until hls.js is installed since that could solve some problems. Additionally, I need to add user photo previews from the backend by finding the API upload endpoint and ensuring we have a route to serve uploaded files, likely stored under storage/registered_faces. Let's dive into the tools!</details></think>

---

### Fixed `/live` 500 (no more `hls.js` module error)
Your Live page was crashing because `hls.js` wasn’t actually installed, so Next couldn’t compile `/live`.

I removed the `hls.js` import and changed Live to:
- **Use native HLS only when the browser supports it (Safari/macOS)**  
- Otherwise **fallback to MJPEG** (so it works immediately without installing anything)

This will make `/live` load again right now.

### Fixed fullscreen “zoomed in” + can’t zoom out
- In fullscreen, the stream now uses **`objectFit: 'contain'`** (instead of always `cover`), so it won’t look pre-zoomed.
- Zoom still works and can zoom out down to 50%.

### Added “View uploaded person photo” in Users
You now get:
- **Avatar thumbnail** (shows the uploaded face photo if present)
- A **View (eye) button** that opens a dialog preview

Backend + frontend changes:
- **Backend**: added `GET /api/v1/users/{user_id}/photo?token=...` to serve the stored `image_path`
- **Frontend**: updated `frontend/src/app/(dashboard)/users/page.tsx` to show thumbnail + preview dialog

---

## Important note about “no persons/objects detected”
Right now, your detection overlay was being drawn in the old MJPEG/OpenCV pipeline (`annotate_frame`). When we move to HLS/FFmpeg, we’re currently streaming the raw camera feed **without the AI overlay**.

So detection may still be happening in the backend, but **it won’t be drawn on the video** unless we:
- reintroduce an overlay pipeline (best: separate AI worker + overlay compositor), or
-

### Fixed: Live feed “stuck” (root cause)
Your backend was defaulting to **`STREAM_MODE=hls`**, but your browser (Chrome) was effectively using the **MJPEG path**, so clicking **Start** wasn’t starting the MJPEG capture → result looked frozen/not live.

I changed the backend default back to **`STREAM_MODE="mjpeg"`** so Live Monitoring works immediately in all browsers again:

- Updated: `backend/app/core/config.py`  
  - **Default `STREAM_MODE` is now `mjpeg`**
  - You can still switch to HLS later with `STREAM_MODE=hls`

After this, restart backend and `/live` should stream properly again.

---

### Fixed: Dashboard stat cards still look “not like cards”
In your screenshot the stats look like floating text/icons and the decorative circles show through. I strengthened the stat card visual separation:

- Updated: `frontend/src/app/(dashboard)/dashboard/page.tsx`
  - Link wrapper is now `display: block; height: 100%`
  - Card now has **stronger border + shadow** and explicit white background
  - Added **focus-visible outline** for accessibility

---

### Notes
- The hydration warning popup in your screenshot is from a browser extension injecting attributes; we already suppressed hydration on `<html>`/`<body>`. If it still appears, try once in an Incognito window (extensions off) to confirm.
- Your `/live` page should no longer 500 from `hls.js` (it’s no longer imported).

If you restart and live still freezes, I’ll adjust the MJPEG endpoint loop timing / buffering next (it’s usually either caching or the backend capture thread not updating frames).

### Fixed: `Failed to start HLS stream (ffmpeg)`
Your logs show **FFmpeg is not installed**, so HLS start was failing:

- `FFmpeg not found. Install ffmpeg or set FFMPEG_PATH.`
- `POST /api/v1/stream/4/start 500`

I updated the backend so that when `STREAM_MODE=hls` but FFmpeg isn’t available, it will **automatically fall back to MJPEG** instead of erroring.

- Updated: `backend/app/api/stream.py`
  - `POST /api/v1/stream/{id}/start` now returns `mode: "mjpeg"` + a warning when it falls back.

### Real-Time Face Detection (overlay)
Your **real-time detection overlay** (faces/objects drawn on frames) currently exists in the **MJPEG/OpenCV path** via `annotate_frame(...)`.  
So with this fallback, Live Monitoring will stream again **and** the overlays/detections will show (assuming RTSP is reachable).

### What to do
- **Restart backend** (stop `./scripts/start-dev.sh` and run again), then try **Start Stream**.

### Optional (best quality)
Install FFmpeg to enable stable HLS later:

```bash
brew install ffmpeg
```

If you want, I can also update `scripts/start-dev.sh` to print a clear message when FFmpeg is missing and `STREAM_MODE=hls`.

### Fixed: “Uploaded photo” not showing
I made the user-photo serving + UI preview more reliable:

- **Backend** (`backend/app/api/users.py`)
  - Now sets the correct `media_type` (jpg/png) when returning the file.
- **Frontend** (`frontend/src/app/(dashboard)/users/page.tsx`)
  - Adds a cache-buster (`&_=${Date.now()}`) so you don’t see stale/broken cached responses after upload.
  - Uses `objectFit: 'contain'` and a fixed height so the image is actually visible.
  - Shows a clear error message if the image fails to load (usually token expired or file missing).

After restarting the backend + refreshing `/users`, click the **eye icon** again.

---

### “Install dependencies automatically” (FFmpeg blocked by Xcode license)
Your terminal shows Homebrew can’t install FFmpeg because macOS is blocking it until you accept the Xcode license:

```bash
sudo xcodebuild -license accept
```

Then you can run:

```bash
brew install ffmpeg
```

I **cannot run `sudo` for you**, but once you run that one-time command, `brew install ffmpeg` will work normally.

---

### Quick check
If photo still doesn’t render after these changes, it’s almost always **login token** related. Do:
- **Logout → Login → retry eye button** (it will refresh the token used in the image URL).

---

Docker is currently building the backend image (it’s a big build). Once it finishes, I’ll run the migrations inside the backend container and then re-check the DB tables and row counts to confirm real-time saving.

---

The Docker **backend image build is still running** (it’s currently downloading/installing a lot of system packages like `gcc`, `libgl`, Mesa, etc.). As soon as this finishes, the command will automatically run:

- `alembic upgrade head` inside the backend container  
- then `\dt` in Postgres to confirm all tables exist

I’m monitoring the build output now; next update will include the **actual table list** and then I’ll verify “real-time saving” by checking row counts after an API call.

---

FFmpeg is now installed on your Mac at **`/usr/local/bin/ffmpeg`**.

### What to do next (so HLS start error goes away)
1. **Restart your backend** with HLS enabled:

```bash
export STREAM_MODE=hls
export FFMPEG_PATH=/usr/local/bin/ffmpeg
./scripts/start-dev.sh backend
```

2. Open `/live` and click **Start Stream** again.

### Important (Real‑Time Face/Object overlay)
- **MJPEG mode** = you get the OpenCV `annotate_frame(...)` overlays (faces/objects drawn).
- **HLS mode** = currently streams the raw feed (no overlay yet) unless we build an “overlay → HLS” pipeline.

So if your goal is *“Real-Time Face Detection overlays in Live Monitoring”*, keep **`STREAM_MODE=mjpeg`** for now (and we’ll harden it to avoid crashes), or I can implement the overlay-to-HLS pipeline next.

If you tell me which you want:
- **A)** MJPEG + overlays (real-time detection visible), or
- **B)** HLS stability (then we add overlay pipeline),
I’ll proceed accordingly.

---

_**User**_

A

---

Great, we’ll stick with **MJPEG + overlays (Option A)**.

Here’s all you need to do:

### 1. Ensure backend is in MJPEG mode
You’re already configured this way in code, but to be explicit when starting locally:

```bash
cd "/Users/devendervutukuru/Desktop/My Projects/Visioryx"
STREAM_MODE=mjpeg ./scripts/start-dev.sh backend
```

(or just `./scripts/start-dev.sh` – the default in `config.py` is now `mjpeg`).

### 2. Start frontend
In a separate terminal:

```bash
cd "/Users/devendervutukuru/Desktop/My Projects/Visioryx/frontend"
npm run dev
```

### 3. Use it
- Go to `/cameras`, make sure a camera has a valid RTSP URL (reachable from your Mac).
- Go to `/live`, click **Start Stream** for that camera.
- You should now see **live video + detection overlays** (faces/objects) coming from the existing OpenCV pipeline.

If you see any new errors (console or backend logs) while starting a stream, paste them and I’ll adjust the MJPEG path further.

---

The log you showed is a **401 Unauthorized** from `/api/v1/users` when `/users` loads; that happens when the token is missing or expired.

I’ve now updated `frontend/src/lib/api.ts` so that **any 401 response automatically logs you out and redirects to `/login`** instead of just throwing an error and spamming 401s:

- Clears `localStorage` token
- Navigates to `/login` if you’re not already there

If you see this again:
- You’ll be redirected to the login screen.
- After logging in, `/users` and other protected pages will load with a valid token and stop returning 401s.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Evaluating project structure**

It seems that the current Visioryx is located at root, but the workspace path is `/Users/.../Desktop/My Projects/Visioryx`. Running git status at `/Users/...` shows untracked directories like `backend/` and `frontend/`, suggesting the repo might be messy. The user pointed to the Visioryx folder, but from a git perspective, it appears untracked. I’m wondering if Visioryx is a nested repo or if there are two copies. Given the confusion, pushing changes could be risky.
**Clarifying git setup**

I need to explain to the user that their git root is the home directory and that Visioryx isn't tracked yet, which means pushing isn’t safe. They could initialize git inside the Visioryx folder or add it as a subdirectory. Since they mentioned pushing to GitHub, I'll recommend creating a separate repo inside Visioryx for that purpose. I’ll provide exact commands for them to initialize a new repo and push, while ensuring I don’t run any destructive operations or execute a push myself.</details></think>

---

You currently have **Git set up at your home folder (`/Users/devendervutukuru`)**, not inside the `Visioryx` project itself, and that home‑level repo:

- Has **local changes in another project** (`Desktop/My Projects/HRMS/...`).
- Treats a lot of things (Downloads, Music, etc.) as **untracked**.
- Does **not** track `Desktop/My Projects/Visioryx` as a clean project repo.

Pushing from here would either:

- Try to push your entire home directory repo (not what you want), or  
- Mix `HRMS` changes with `Visioryx` in the same commit.

Because of that, I should **not** run `git add` / `git push` for you from `/Users/devendervutukuru`.

### How to correctly push just Visioryx to GitHub

Do this once:

```bash
cd "/Users/devendervutukuru/Desktop/My Projects/Visioryx"

# If there's no repo here yet:
git init

# Add only this project’s files
git add .

# First commit
git commit -m "Initial commit for Visioryx (dashboard, backend, streaming)"
```

Then on GitHub:

1. Create a new empty repo (e.g. `visioryx`).
2. Follow the instructions they show, e.g.:

```bash
git remote add origin git@github.com:YOUR_USERNAME/visioryx.git
git branch -M main
git push -u origin main
```

From then on, you’ll work **inside** `Visioryx`:

```bash
cd "/Users/devendervutukuru/Desktop/My Projects/Visioryx"
# After making changes:
git status
git add <files>
git commit -m "Describe your change"
git push
```

If you’d like, I can help you write the first commit message(s) once this per‑project repo is initialized.
